threadTitle: Things that instantly reveal you just moved here
body: You still think bus timetables are a suggestion, rather than a cruel joke played by TfL.
Caught the 133 last night and a bloke was trying to sell half a sandwich. Said it was ‘gourmet’. No takers, just awkward laughter.

threadTitle: When you knew you were actually living here
body: When I saw a couple of kids playing soccer in the middle of the street, dodging cars like it was a game. It just clicked—this is home.

threadTitle: Things Berlin does that nowhere else does
body: Only in Friedrichshain can you find a bar with a trampoline in the middle. Jumping while sipping your beer feels so surreal.

threadTitle: Real signs someone is from here vs just working here
body: You know someone’s local when they casually mention the BART delays like it's morning weather—30,000 times and counting, yet still ride the train.

threadTitle: Muni moments that define this city
body: Last week I saw a guy in a tuxedo get on the 47. He didn't bat an eye at the bus's 'out of service' sign. Just another Friday night in SF.

threadTitle: Things tourists get wrong about living here
body: Tourists think 'siesta' means everyone’s asleep. Nah, it’s when locals just enjoy a quiet café and some shade, not a nap!

threadTitle: How to spot a recent arrival
body: They still stare up at the buildings like they’re in a museum, missing the locals darting past with their café con leche in hand.

threadTitle: Things that instantly reveal you just moved here
body: You call a 'baguette' a 'French stick' and look puzzled when someone mentions the 73 bus doesn't run after 11.30pm on weekends.
Caught a ride on the 59 last night; a guy was singing to the moon like it was his best mate. Only in Brixton, right?

threadTitle: When you knew you were actually living here
body: I knew I was really living in Neukölln when I saw a guy selling handmade döner wraps from a cargo bike outside the Kottbusser Tor U-Bahn station at midnight.

threadTitle: Things Berlin does that nowhere else does
body: Only in Kreuzberg do you find döner shops open at 4 AM, serving up fresh bread and spicy sauce to night owls coming home from clubs.

threadTitle: Real signs someone is from here vs just working here
body: You know someone’s a local when they can name all the taco trucks on 16th without hesitation, but a newbie stares blankly at a menu board.

threadTitle: Muni moments that define this city
body: Last week, I watched a Muni bus roll past three times, each driver waving at a guy playing guitar on the corner. His serenade never stopped.

threadTitle: Things tourists get wrong about living here
body: Tourists think Gràcia is just about Gaudí, but the real heart is in its small plazas, where locals gather for vermut and laughter, not selfies.

threadTitle: How to spot a recent arrival
body: You can always tell the newbies by their reluctance to embrace the 1:30 lunch hour—still holding fast to noon meal times, bless their hearts.
